author: @ThisRandomUsername
text: In a previous job we'd wind coils around rods of ferrite. Basically to get a decent flux path above 20kHz you need to use ferrite. For DC, it'll probably work much the same as a chunk of iron, but for lower frequency AC you'll want to use laminated sheets because they produce less heat as the flux passes through them.

author: @jamesharris5158
text: I would be interested to see the difference between different "grades" of core. The different grades lead to different inductance per turn when they are made into inductors which is a result of the different makeup of the core material.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text: I use those toroids when I am building super joule thief or joule ringer devices. The have amazing high frequency resonation properties, and I am easily getting 50+v DC from a standard 0.5v PV cell on the desk under the desk lamp.

author: @totherarf
text:
The toroid will work in the way you have it set up .... but when used in a motor / generator you may find heating becomes a problem! That is why the laminations in a "normal" transformer are set up the way they are to reduce eddy currents.

An equivalent, easy and cheap method for a core in this situation could be simply bundling up some pin nails with capton tape and winding the coil in the same way as the toroid!

I have to say the whole concept of magnetic multiplying and reducing is a fascinating one! It will reduce the problem of inrush current or cogging (same thing one for a motor one for a generator). Adjusting the imput voltage would also adjust the power of the device, which is not the case for "normal" motors, and could allow rotation of a generator at low windspeed!

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text: I wonder if the powdered iron reduces the eddy currents as well or better.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @technosaurus3805 I would assume it does .... but it would also atenuate the magnetic field. The trick is to have a complete path of ferrous metal along the lines of flux but to have none at right angles to it!

replies.author: @jamesharris5158
replies.text: Eddy currents are not a problem in these cores as they are designed for transformer and inductor use. The particles of iron are electrically insulated from one another.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @jamesharris5158  It is my understanding they are for small inductors and very small transformers.... but I could be wrong ;0) If they are composed of magnetically separated particles they will not be a good conduit for magnetic flux (compared with a lamination)! There are Toroidal transformer cores to be sure but they are not a toroid themselves and have end caps of iron.

replies.author: @jamesharris5158
replies.text:
@totherarf They are designed for many different applications and have different formulations. As a result of the different makeup, they have different permeability and suitability for different jobs.
I would think that a high permeability core type like a mix 75 would be suitable for motors. It's easy enough when you order these things from a reputable supplier to know what you are getting, but if you are salvaging from dead equipment then it is slightly involved to measure the permeability, but it can be done.

author: @garethb5729
text:
When you take an iron core and wrap a coil around, it imprints the field. Replace the coil for another and it's efficiency decreases. 
The iron core becomes like second hand shoes. They work but they don't fit right. 

Completely none related. Lol. 

Awesome as always Rob. God bless. Happy Easter mate.

author: @d11628
text: I have never seen a torid wound over the top, most of the ones I have seen have the wire wound around the inside to outside. Probably much more of a pain to make.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: A pain ..... and the field is at right angles to the coil so aligns with the toroid making it an inductor rather than an electromagnet!

replies.author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
replies.text:
it is, but I wonder if you could wind a coil on  a straight bit of flexible tubing, bend it around like a jump ring, fill it with powdered iron, and glue it together into a ring, or just take several small bobbin coils, soldered together in sequence as you go, and feed them on an Iron jump ring and bend it shut?

I guess you'd have to separate all the wires at the ends, like leave a tail at either end with each pass, and wind those all together to keep the flow all going the same direction?  dunno.  it's a thought.
